domain_summary: The AI domain in early 2026 is characterized by major advances in open-source models, with Alibaba's Qwen-3.5 (Feb 2026) featuring 397B MoE parameters and Meta's Llama 4 Maverick (April 2025) establishing multimodal benchmarks. Enterprise adoption has reached a tipping point with 76% of organizations using open-source LLMs, driven by cost efficiency and customization needs. Mixture-of-Experts architectures like Qwen-3.5 offer computational efficiency through sparse activation but face memory bandwidth bottlenecks and routing overhead challenges. Despite widespread deployment, official benchmark disclosure is asymmetric across major providers, with comprehensive inference performance data remaining largely unpublished by model creators.

claims.claim: Qwen-3.5 officially released February 16-17, 2026 by Alibaba Cloud with 397-billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts architecture
claims.notes: Confirmed across multiple authoritative sources including official Alibaba blog and major news outlets

claims.claim: Llama 4 Maverick released April 5, 2025 with 17 billion active parameters in 400B MoE framework and multimodal capabilities
claims.notes: Official Meta release confirmed in April 2025, widely deployed in early 2026

claims.claim: Llama 4 Maverick features 1 million token context window
claims.notes: Conflicted by Oracle documentation citing 512,000 tokens

claims.claim: Llama 4 Maverick features 512,000 token context window according to Oracle documentation
claims.notes: Oracle official documentation conflicts with other sources citing 1M tokens

claims.claim: 76% of enterprises using LLMs choose open-source options, often running them alongside proprietary models (as of November 2025)
claims.notes: Databricks State of AI report data showing enterprise adoption trends

claims.claim: Over 50% of organizations implement open source across their AI technology stack (2025 data)
claims.notes: Indicates mainstream adoption beyond early-adopter phase

claims.claim: Qwen-3.5 uses 397B total MoE parameters with only 17B active parameters per token
claims.notes: Core MoE architecture specification confirmed across multiple sources

claims.claim: MoE sparse activation reduces memory bandwidth and compute FLOPs compared to dense models with equivalent parameter counts
claims.notes: Established MoE efficiency principle confirmed by IEEE research

claims.claim: Routing overhead is a significant latency penalty for MoE models not present in dense architectures
claims.notes: Fundamental MoE challenge identified by multiple 2026 sources

claims.claim: MoE serving requires preloading all experts into memory despite sparse activation, creating memory bandwidth bottleneck
claims.notes: Critical MoE inefficiency at inference time

claims.claim: Llama 4 Maverick MMLU Pro score is 59.6
claims.notes: Official Groq documentation and Meta model card, but MMLU Pro variant, not standard MMLU

claims.claim: Qwen-3.5-397B official benchmark scores from Alibaba NOT publicly available in verified sources
claims.notes: Despite extensive searching, no official Alibaba technical report with specific benchmark scores found

claims.claim: No official Llama 4 Maverick ARC or TruthfulQA benchmark scores located in primary sources
claims.notes: Meta documentation focuses on MMLU Pro, MATH, HumanEval; ARC and TruthfulQA absent

claims.claim: GLM-4.7 achieves 73.8% on SWE-bench coding benchmark from THUDM
claims.notes: Chinese open-source model performance, but original benchmark source not directly verified

claims.claim: Meta has officially published MMLU-Pro score (59.6) for Llama 4 Maverick via model card and documentation
claims.notes: Verified in official Meta model card on GitHub and Hugging Face

claims.claim: Google has officially published benchmark scores for Gemma 3 (27B variant, March 25, 2025) including MMLU-Pro 67.5
claims.notes: Gemma 3 technical report includes benchmark evaluation metrics

claims.claim: Anthropic has NOT released open-source models in 2025-2026; only proprietary Claude models exist
claims.notes: Claude models are proprietary, not open-source

claims.claim: Llama 4 Maverick baseline inference specifications: $0.31 per 1M input tokens, $0.85 per 1M output tokens via Artificial Analysis
claims.notes: Independent benchmark aggregator tracking 11+ cloud providers, but compiled from APIs not official Meta specs

claims.claim: AWS Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex AI, Azure, and Alibaba Cloud all offer Llama 4 Maverick and/or Qwen-3.5 as managed services
claims.notes: Verified across multiple official cloud provider documentation

claims.claim: Llama 4 Maverick via Groq: 350 tokens per second throughput with ~4,900 ms latency
claims.notes: Groq specialized inference provider specifications from December 1, 2025

claims.claim: Qwen-3.5-397B on Cerebras Wafer: >1000 tokens per second
claims.notes: Specialized hardware performance, lacks detail on context length and batch size

claims.claim: No official MLPerf or standardized LMSys Chatbot Arena inference speed benchmarks published for Qwen-3.5-397B or Llama 4 Maverick as of February 2026
claims.notes: Standard benchmarking platforms measure capability/quality, not infrastructure performance metrics

claims.claim: Qwen-3.5-397B is available via API pricing on Alibaba Cloud but specific latency/throughput metrics not publicly disclosed
claims.notes: Alibaba Cloud provides token pricing ($0.115 per 1M input tokens) but no quantitative performance metrics

contradictions.description: Llama 4 Maverick context window size - 1 million vs 512,000 tokens
contradictions.resolution: unresolved - suggests specification variations across deployment platforms or versioning differences

knowledge_gaps.gap: Official MMLU, ARC, and TruthfulQA benchmark scores for both Qwen-3.5 and Llama 4 Maverick
Direct inference latency and cost-per-token comparisons between Qwen-3.5 MoE and Llama 4 on identical hardware
Exact VRAM requirements for Qwen-3.5-397B vs Llama 4 Maverick at different batch sizes
Official inference performance benchmarks from Meta and Alibaba for their respective models
Standardized comparison methodology for inference performance across cloud providers
Market share distribution between different open-source models in early 2026
Complete benchmark disclosures in official technical reports from Alibaba, Google, and Microsoft
Context length effects on latency for both models with stratified measurements
Hardware-specific optimization analysis for MoE routing efficiency across different GPU architectures
